resources.string: Detects which app is in the foreground and reads the URL bar in your browser so Defang can show a pause screen before watched apps or adult websites open. Defang does not log, store, or transmit your browsing history.
This is a speed bump. Not a wall.

Some apps will block your phone completely. Some will go full grayscale through developer settings. This is not that. You can still open Instagram. You can still override the timer. Nothing here is impossible to bypass.

That is deliberate. A wall creates pressure. Pressure demands release. You've seen this: Instagram's own time limit ships with a "15 more minutes" button that you tap without thinking. The limit exists, but the exit is so frictionless that it trains you to ignore the limit, not respect it.

This app puts a speed bump in front of the reflex. It makes the unconscious open a conscious one. What you do after that is up to you.
Not a willpower app. Not a parental control.

You probably already know that Instagram is engineered to keep you scrolling. You probably already know that pull-to-refresh mimics a slot machine. Knowing this has not been enough, because the systems working against you are not aimed at your knowledge — they are aimed at the part of your brain that acts before you think.

This app is not asking you to try harder. It is inserting a small pause between the impulse and the action. That pause is where your prefrontal cortex lives.

It also does not touch your developer settings, does not require a rooted phone, and does not apply system-wide grayscale. Your camera, your maps, and your calculator work exactly as before.
Cue → routine → reward. They own the cue.

Every habit runs on a three-part loop: a cue triggers a routine that delivers a reward. Apps have industrialized the cue. The red badge, the notification at exactly the right moment, the muscle memory of unlocking your phone when you're bored — all of it is a designed cue that launches the routine before you've decided anything.

This app targets two points in the loop. Before you open: it interrupts the cue with a pause and asks what you actually want. After you close: it tries to hand you a different reward — something real, physical, and yours — before the loop resets.

It cannot touch what's inside the app. But the cue and the landing are up for grabs.
\"Tidy the coffee table\" is not a joke.

When you reach for your phone out of boredom or restlessness, you are in a low-energy, low-motivation state. A suggestion like "clean the bathroom" in that state does not create action. It creates a second reason to open Instagram.

Small tasks work because they are completable. Completing something — anything — produces real dopamine: the kind that persists, not the kind that fades in three seconds. "Put one thing back where it belongs" is achievable in 20 seconds. Once you've done it, you're standing up, you've moved, and the loop has been interrupted by something that required your body, not just your thumb.

The tasks are not there to make you productive. They are there to give the reward system something honest to work with.
Let\'s set it up. Two minutes.

Pick which apps get the speed bump. Set how long you want to spend in them before the timer kicks in. Choose when you want notifications from those apps to arrive.

You can change everything later. The defaults are reasonable starting points, not recommendations.

If you've set a Bedtime or Do Not Disturb schedule on your phone, this app will automatically increase friction during those hours.
What are you opening this for?
Why do you need more time?
You\'ve used your one extension for today.
Apps that trigger the speed bump
Time before the end card appears
Time before the app can be reopened
The full content library with sources
These apps are watched by default. Adjust if needed.
Defang needs this to show the pause screen before watched apps open. Without it, the core feature doesn\'t work.

Defang needs to know when a watched app opens, and reads the browser\'s address bar to recognise adult domains. It never reads page content, messages, or anything you type — only the foreground app and the URL in the address field.
Already enabled — you can turn it off here
Samsung\'s battery management can kill background services, which would stop the session timer working. Tap to exclude Defang from battery optimisation.
Needed to show your time-in-app history.
Gate delay, max session length, and cool-down
Applies to all watched apps unless you change them individually.
Watched apps render in black and white during a session

Grayscale needs a one-time permission that can only be granted from a computer. Connect the phone over USB with USB debugging enabled and run:

adb shell pm grant %1$s android.permission.WRITE_SECURE_SETTINGS

Then come back here to turn grayscale on.

The colors in social apps are not accidental. Red badges and saturated feed colors are chosen because the brain reads them as reward. In black and white the content is the same, but the reward signal is gone — the pull to keep scrolling weakens measurably. Your camera, maps, and the rest of the phone keep their colors.
Notifications from watched apps are muted: gray, silent, no preview

Notifications are the platforms\' strongest tool for pulling you back: brand-red color, sound, vibration, and urgency language (\"X is waiting for you\") — often bulk sends dressed up as personal messages. Defang replaces them with one gray, silent summary per app, with no content preview. You know something is waiting — without the bait. Tapping the summary opens the app through its front door, not the deep link the notification wanted to send you to.
Required for neutral notifications — grant Defang access to notifications
Nothing but the status and navigation bars
Just the tidbit of the day
Clock, date, and the tidbit of the day
Tasks you add are suggested on the end card alongside the built-in ones
No tasks yet. Add something small and completable — 20 seconds is enough.
Search by app or package name
Hidden apps disappear from the app drawer but still show up when you search there.
Muted summaries of notifications from watched apps
Weekly time in watched apps — evidence, not guilt
No watched-app sessions in the last 7 days.
%1$d of %2$d declared-intent sessions ran past 8 minutes (%3$d%%).
No data for the 7 days before.
Measured from Defang\'s own session log — gated sessions only. All data stays on this device.

From September 2026, Google blocks installing apps from developers who have not registered with Google, paid a fee, and handed over government ID. Defang's developer will not do that — the requirement is the same class of control this app exists to push back against.

What it means for you: this installed copy keeps working, unaffected. Installing future updates will require either F-Droid, or Google's multi-step "advanced flow" (Developer Mode plus a 24-hour waiting period). That flow runs through Google Play Services and Google can tighten it at any time.

More at keepandroidopen.org — in a browser, when you choose to.
What September 2026 means for updating this app
Small bars under the clock — only shown on days with watched-app time
Today\'s time in each watched app vs your limit
